name: Little Cottonwood
short_description: BLM public land in the Wasatch Range offering free rockhounding for quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. Beautiful alpine setting with accessible mineral-rich outcrops.
description:
Little Cottonwood is a scenic canyon in the Wasatch Range offering free public rockhounding on BLM land near Salt Lake City. The area is renowned for collecting quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens found in granitic outcrops and talus slopes typical of this Precambrian metamorphic and igneous terrain. Rockhounders can explore accessible hiking trails and canyon walls where weathering has exposed fresh mineral surfaces.

The canyon sits within the Wasatch Range, a north-south trending mountain range formed during the Laramide Orogeny and further shaped by Basin and Range extension. The granitic bedrock here crystallized during the Precambrian and contains excellent feldspathic and quartz-rich zones. The high elevation and steep canyon walls provide natural mineral exposure, making specimen hunting productive without extensive digging.

Access is straightforward from Salt Lake City, and the scenic setting makes this ideal for combining rockhounding with hiking and photography. Most collecting occurs on talus slopes and along canyon wash banks where specimens have naturally weathered free. The area is popular with intermediate rockhounders seeking quality quartz and feldspar without the commitment of a fee dig site.

Best results come from examining outcrops on north-facing slopes and searching talus piles at canyon confluence points. Spring and fall weather makes extended collecting sessions comfortable, and the high elevation provides natural cooling during warmer months.
history: Little Cottonwood Canyon was carved by glaciation and stream erosion through Precambrian metamorphic and granitic rocks that crystallized over 1.5 billion years ago. The Wasatch Range underwent major uplift during the Laramide Orogeny and continuing extension, exposing these ancient mineral-rich formations that attract rockhounders today.
tips: Search the talus slopes and loose rock piles on both sides of the canyon, particularly where small streams have washed finer material away - these naturally sorted collections expose fresh quartz and feldspar faces. Look for feldspathic granite outcrops with visible pink orthoclase crystals; use a hand lens to identify quality specimens before collecting. Bring a pry bar or small hammer to carefully extract specimens from loose talus rather than attempting to chip the solid bedrock. Early morning or late afternoon light at this elevation helps spot the sparkle of fresh crystal faces on white quartz and translucent feldspar.
terrain_notes: Alpine canyon terrain at 7,000-8,500 feet elevation with steep granitic walls, active stream flow, and dense conifer forest mixed with aspen groves. Talus slopes are rocky with loose angular debris requiring sturdy footwear. Hiking distances from parking are 0.5 to 2 miles depending on target outcrops. Terrain is uneven and exposed to sudden weather changes at this elevation.
written_directions: From downtown Salt Lake City, take I-15 south to exit 4A and merge onto UT-210 (Little Cottonwood Road) heading east. Follow UT-210 for approximately 8 miles up Little Cottonwood Canyon. Turn right at the signed trailhead parking areas along the canyon road. Multiple pullouts and parking areas exist between mile markers 5 and 8 of UT-210. Park at any BLM-designated pullout and hike into the canyon walls or explore talus slopes directly accessible from the parking areas.
road_conditions: Paved highway (UT-210) in good condition year-round, though winter snow may require tire chains. Road is well-maintained with regular pullouts.
parking_notes: Multiple BLM pullouts and small gravel parking areas along UT-210 throughout the canyon. Capacity varies from 2-15 vehicles per pullout. High-use areas may fill during summer weekends.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No collection from active mining claims. Respect private property boundaries. Pack out all trash and leave no trace.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No active digging in solid bedrock
Respect canyon vegetation
Pack out all waste
nearest_services: Salt Lake City, approximately 12 miles northwest. Gas, food, water, and lodging available throughout the city.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky talus slopes and canyon terrain require sturdy hiking boots and reasonable fitness. No wheelchair accessibility. Steep hillsides and loose rock present ongoing hazards. Stream crossings may be necessary depending on selected routes. Not suitable for limited mobility or young children without close supervision.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at Little Cottonwood near Salt Lake City?
faq.answer: Little Cottonwood is best known for quartz and feldspar specimens found in granitic outcrops and talus slopes. You can also collect mica and beryl crystals. The high-elevation Wasatch Range bedrock is ideal for finding quality white quartz and pink orthoclase feldspar on the canyon walls and loose rock piles.

faq.question: Is a permit required to rockhound at Little Cottonwood Canyon, Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Little Cottonwood is BLM public land open to recreational rockhounding under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act. You can collect free of charge, but must follow the 25 lbs per day limit and personal use rules.

faq.question: How far is Little Cottonwood from Salt Lake City and what are the road conditions?
faq.answer: Little Cottonwood is only 12 miles southeast of downtown Salt Lake City. Take UT-210 (Little Cottonwood Road) from I-15 south, exit 4A. The paved highway is well-maintained year-round, though winter snow may require chains. Multiple parking pullouts line the canyon.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Little Cottonwood Canyon?
faq.answer: June through September is ideal. The high elevation (7,000-8,500 feet) makes this season perfect for avoiding extreme heat while still having comfortable hiking conditions. Winter brings heavy snow and avalanche danger, making spring access unpredictable.

faq.question: Can beginners rockhound at Little Cottonwood, and is it family-friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, Little Cottonwood is beginner and family-friendly for children 8 and up. The talus slopes and canyon setting require only basic hiking skills, and specimens are accessible without advanced tools. Multiple short hikes make it easy to adjust difficulty to your group.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do you need to collect quartz and feldspar at Little Cottonwood?
faq.answer: A small hammer, pry bar, hand lens, and sturdy gloves are helpful. Most quality specimens can be collected from loose talus without extensive digging. Bring plenty of water (no reliable water source in canyon), hiking boots, and a backpack. Since the 25 lb daily limit applies, you won't need a large collection bag.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Salt Lake City. Find quartz, feldspar, and mica in the Wasatch Range. No permit required. Personal use only.
